Makepeace Business System
Module 3 • Making A Big Splash
Transcript 	Page  of 23
The Total Package 	www.makepeacetotalpackage.com
I want to welcome everybody. I think this should be a fun session today. We are
continuing our conversation about partnering. Today, I’m trying to make sure that in
each session we’re making sure that we have concepts for both sides of the partnering
equation, both the company owner and the copywriter – that we’re talking about more
than just deal making but also the actual work that needs to get done in order to make
partnering work.
Last time we talked about doing the deal and getting the contracts done. This time we’re
going to try to take the next step a little bit. We’re going to look at some of the things that I
do when I come into a company and begin working with them for the first time.
The things that we’re going to talk about today are especially important for business
owners as well as copywriters, because a lot of business owners will look at these
kinds of recommendations and say, “Wow, you know, I should be doing all of this for my
company anyway.” So I think there will be value there.
Essentially, we’re going to be talking about how to find the hidden value in a company.
That’s how we’re making a big splash as copywriters or rainmakers as we go to work with
a company.
The most critical days of that new relationship really are at the very beginning, at the time
when the business owner is thinking, “Wow, I’m giving this guy a chunk of my growth or
a chunk of my profits in terms of royalties or commissions. I sure hope that was a good
decision.”
And, of course, that is still to be seen. And the copywriter or rainmaker is thinking, “Wow,
I sure hope I can make this company grow and that the revenues are realized from this
and will make all of this worthwhile.”
There are other reasons why the first 60 days of a partnership are so important. I’m going
to speak primarily from a copywriter’s standpoint today, because most of us that are on
the call are copywriters. First of all, it is essential, really, for copywriters to make sure
that the first 60 days onboard really blow the company owner away in terms of results for
several reasons.
The first is credibility. You’re creating a foundation here, and first impressions are
everything. If you can get into the company owner’s mind the picture of how much money
is to be made together as partners in this deal, you’ll have more credibility and the new
ideas that you bring to him after that first 60 days will have more traction with him and will
be more likely to be implemented.
Another reason is it gives you power in the relationship. One of the problems that
invariably arise between rainmakers and their clients is the staff. You’re the stranger from
out of town – you’re the outsider to this company. The people within the company have
been busy building their own kingdoms, advancing their careers, and all of a sudden
here’s a guy from outside who’s coming in with ideas and copy and all of these other
things. So from time to time you’ll encounter resistance from the staff.

-- 1 of 23 --

Makepeace Business System
Module 3 • Making A Big Splash
Transcript 	Page 2 of 23
The Total Package 	www.makepeacetotalpackage.com
If you’ve established credibility with the company owner right off the bat in the first 60
days by hitting some home runs, the business owner is going to be more likely to be
your advocate to his staff and to ensure that your ideas and suggestions area seriously
considered and implemented.
Another reason is obvious - the money. You want to generate the greatest amount of
money in the shortest amount of time for both of you. That’s how we keep score. That’s
how we measure.
It’s important for the company owner, but for the copywriter or rainmaker, the marketing
consultant or business builder that comes into a company for the first time, you’re dealing
with a virgin list in many cases – a customer file that has not had optimally conceived,
designed, and executed promotions sent to them before.
It’s at that moment in time when that list is likely to be the most productive, that you’re
most likely to get the biggest amount of money per name on that file. It’s a key opportunity
for the rainmaker to go in there and make a lot of money really, really fast. So credibility,
power, and money for both of you. That first 60 days is really critical.
When I go into a company and I look around, the first thing I recognize is that in every
company I go to there are tremendous opportunities. Some of them are from things they
should be doing that they don’t. Some are from things that they’re doing poorly or not at
all. Others are unrealized opportunities in the case of new products, stronger promotions,
and so forth.
We’ve talked about this before. There are six primary growth engines in every company.
1. 	You can go into a company and create new products or entire new product lines.
That will add to the bottom line.
2. 	You can go in there, and you can bring in hundreds of thousands of new
customers. Obviously, a big revenue increaser.
3. 	You can create campaigns and long-term strategies – annual strategies to cause
existing customers to order more frequently.
4. 	You can create strategies and promotions designed to help each customer make
a larger purchase each time they order, thereby increasing the overall value of the
file and, of course, the company itself.
5. 	You can create promotions to keep customers with you longer and keep them
active longer.
6. 	You can improve the profit margin of the company by lowering the cost on some
of the things they do including marketing, fulfilment, premiums, and in other things
too.

-- 2 of 23 --

Makepeace Business System
Module 3 • Making A Big Splash
Transcript 	Page 3 of 23
The Total Package 	www.makepeacetotalpackage.com
For example, for me, customer lifetime extension is really important. A lot of guys in my
industry, for example, will pay $169 to buy a new subscriber to a financial newsletter.
They essentially go into the mail and spend $169 for each new customer that they bring
in. They’re looking to break even on that first sale, but still that $169 is a risk.
Of course, that’s a rule of thumb that varies some, but you get the general idea. Very few
of them are willing to pay $169 to keep a customer, so there are tremendous opportunities
in customer retention.
Before I move on, let me just say – and I’ve written about this, so I’m not going to spend a
lot of time on it – you can imagine that you don’t have to be the greatest copywriter in the
world and you don’t have to be the greatest marketing genius in the world to double the
sales of a company very quickly. You can see how if you just did each one of these things
20% better - if you helped execute 20% more new products in the year ahead.
Let’s say the guy’s got five newsletters now and you introduce a new idea for one new
newsletter. You bring in 20% more customers, which could easily be done even if the guy
was just marketing through direct mail by increasing the frequency of the mailing and the
response rate on those mailings. You increase the number of times each customer orders
per year by 20%. You increase the average order size by 20%.
Do you see what’s happening here? You keep each customer with you 20% longer and at
the same time lower the cost of fulfilling on each order by 20%. If you do all of that, you’ve
got more than a double in a year.
It takes a systematic approach to working through the company and consciously saying,
“Okay, how can we expand the product line? How can we get 20% more customers?”
Go through this list. If you’re doing this and doing it every single month, you’re going
to get there and you’re going to create miraculous results. Pretty soon you’ll be writing
ezines about how you quadrupled a company in a year.
Now the things that we just looked at really kind of fall into three categories:
INCREMENTAL IMPROVEMENT
The first one is Incremental Improvement. Here, I’m talking about testing. It’s constant
improvement. This process never stops. The thing about testing is, it’s kind of like how
elephants mate. There’s a lot of thrashing around and loud noises, and 22 months later
you get results. You get a baby elephant. That’s what testing is like.
A lot of the tests that you do are going to be failures. There’s no guarantee that any test
is going to work. In fact, the better your original promotions were or the better your initial
strategy was, the fewer tests are going to actually work. Most tests cost money in direct
mail or any place that you have to buy your media, for example, they definitely cost
money. Even on the internet they cost money, because there’s lost opportunity costs and

-- 3 of 23 --

Makepeace Business System
Module 3 • Making A Big Splash
Transcript 	Page  of 23
The Total Package 	www.makepeacetotalpackage.com
so forth.
Incremental improvement is absolutely necessary, but it’s a long-term project and tends
to be a substantial amount of time before you begin seeing the really huge results or
benefits from all of that testing.
Now this is in a company. A lot of guys will talk about testing and the Taguchi method and
all that kind of stuff. They’re talking about kind of one-shot internet based or web-based
promotions – testing a headline on a promotion piece, that kind of thing. Even in that
case, if you test A headline today, it’s going to be three, four, five months before you’re
going to roll it out at most companies. There’s a time factor there.
Working a lot on testing in month one or month two with a new partner isn’t going to blow
him out of his socks in terms of dollars to the bottom line.
ADDITIVE IMPROVEMENT
The second kind of improvement is additive improvement. This is kind of a layering-on
effect. This is where, as time goes on, these things begin to become a very significant
improvement to the bottom line.
We’re going to get into this more deeply in a moment, but you know, you go into a
company and they’ve got a great direct mail business, but they don’t have a web
presence. So you add another layer. You add the web presence. Later on you add a new
product or two. Later on you add a television infomercial.
So it’s additive and it tends to be sequential. It takes time. It’s not like incremental
improvement in terms of testing where it’s an on ongoing process. With additive
improvement, there tends to be a burst right at the beginning of the process.
I’ll give you an example. That probably means I’ll have to jump over a slide later, but I feel
like I’m being a little vague here.
I went into a health company. They had no website, no ezine, no catalog, and no package
stuffers to go out when they shipped their supplements. We spent probably three months
creating all of those things for the company.
We gave them a catalog first. Then we used that copy to create a website. Then we
created an ezine and brought in an editorial person to do the daily ezines to their
customers. The ezines always led back to their website so people could order.
All of that was like 2-1/2 years ago. This month, I received a $50,000 royalty – a little bit
from the catalog, a little bit from the website, etc. and it just goes on like that. Every single
month, I get a royalty from this work I did two or three years ago.
This kind of stuff is cool, because you do the work once, and you’ll have some updates
from time to time, but the work is substantially done. It’s like a little money machine sitting

-- 4 of 23 --

Makepeace Business System
Module 3 • Making A Big Splash
Transcript 	Page  of 23
The Total Package 	www.makepeacetotalpackage.com
there generating dollars pretty much forever. It’s almost like an annuity.
Whereas if you do a direct mail package – if your only relationship with a client, if you’re
not partnering and you’re just doing that front-end direct mail package, you know what’s
going to happen? You write a promotion, you have to be the strong control, and that’s a
big challenge.
He’s going to throw a couple other copywriters at that control at the same time, and you
also have to beat them – there’s another challenge. As soon as you get the control, if
you’re fortunate enough or smart enough to, he now is going to hire other copywriters
every month or every two months or every quarter to try and beat your control. Every
single one of those things means there’s a good chance that your royalties from that
control are going to stop like now.
That doesn’t happen with this additive improvement thing. Catalogs, websites, package
stuffers, billing stuffers, thank you series, renewal series, or customer retention series
– those type of things stay in place for a long, long time. He’s not throwing copywriters at
them every month, and they’ll continue genning out the royalties for a long time.
EXPLOSIVE IMPROVEMENT
The next one is explosive improvement. Those are the things where you can walk into the
company and 60 days later walk home with a whole lot of money. We’re going to look at
each of these now.
Also when we get to the end of the presentation, just know that this is foundational for
next month’s webinar, which is going to go into greater depth on the first 60 days on each
of, I think there are six, these things that I would usually do when going into a company.
Let me just say that from my standpoint, these types of webinars are a little difficult to
do because there are some of you that are trying to sell a new car that your partner
has invented. Other people are selling cosmetics. Other people are selling real estate
programs.
I’m keenly aware that not everyone on this call is working in the same industry as I am,
so giving very specific examples is difficult. So you’re going to have to think your way
through this process, and our goal today is just to give you some guidelines for doing that.
In the first 60 days our mission is to produce maximum money with minimum work in the
shortest time. The one thing I want to point out here is item #2 there – the “with minimum
work” part.
It doesn’t matter to me if I have to work 18 hours a day for the first couple of months with
a new company. What I want, though, is I don’t want to have to rely on my new partner’s
staff to implement or execute the lion’s share of the work that I do. If the ball gets dropped
anywhere, that’s going to reflect poorly on me, because ultimately I’m going to be judged
on the bottom line at the end of this period of time.

-- 5 of 23 --

Makepeace Business System
Module 3 • Making A Big Splash
Transcript 	Page  of 23
The Total Package 	www.makepeacetotalpackage.com
I want as much as I possibly can have under my own direct control. And I think that’s an
important discussion to have with the company owner at the outset just so everyone is
clear on this.
I have a client that I’m doing a product launch for right now, and the first thing I told him
was, “Before I go ahead and do this launch for you, I want it clear that it’s not going to
happen on your server – it’s going to happen on mine.”
That way I don’t have to wait for his staff. I know that my work will always be their lowest
priority, the things that I want done, because their boss is standing right there asking them
to do other things. I want to have the maximum amount of control and, of course, in the
shortest amount of time.
Those of you who have done this kind of thing before or have been on a number of my
webinars, or even read a number of Total Package issues, probably had a really good
idea where we were going with this. But I think it’s a good idea to kind of work through the
whole process.
INCREMENTAL IMPROVEMENT
Okay, so let’s start with the incremental improvement, which is testing. As you’re going
through the company and taking a look at the opportunities, it’s really good to actually
do this in a systematic way and create a checklist. Record every thought, every idea,
everything you should test, ever new idea you have for him, or whatever. That way,
you can keep coming back to that every month as you’re looking for, “What’s my next
project?”
Test New Customer Acquisition Strategies
You have to look at how your new partner is producing new customers now. There’s
really only a couple of ways that it’s done. One way is the one-shot promos. That would
be like he runs an infomercial. He has to spend X number of dollars to put that on the air.
That infomercial then produces X number of new customers. Maybe he runs an ad in a
newspaper, or does a mailing, or does a radio spot.
These one-shot promotions basically, because they have a cost associated with them, in
most cases are going to be a pitch of some kind. It is going to be an offer for a product
because you’re hoping that you’ll get enough money back from that sale to pay for the
cost of running the promotion.
The things that you’ll want to test there, if he’s doing one-shot promotions, is you’re going
to want to look at media – and we’re going to look at media more in depth in a minute.
You’ll want to test other media. You’re going to want to test the offer – and we’re going to
look a whole bunch of offer structures.
You’re going to want to test price, because we’ve seen a lower price produce better

-- 6 of 23 --

Makepeace Business System
Module 3 • Making A Big Splash
Transcript 	Page  of 23
The Total Package 	www.makepeacetotalpackage.com
results in customer acquisition and we’ve seen a higher price on some occasions produce
better bottom-line results and better return on investment on new-customer promotions.
You’re going to want to look at testing those things.
Now the other kind of model really is a process model. That’s more like the internet model
where you have an ezine. You offer free reports for people who sign-up for the ezine.
You drive people to the page offering those free reports with a pay-per-click campaign
or banner ads, or you rent an email list and you send out an email to a whole bunch of
people offering a free report.
There are costs associated with doing those things. With pay-per-click, you’ve got to
pay per click. So your hope there is that over time, not on the first transaction, which is
generally an opt-in to a free ezine, but over time you will receive a sale, a purchase from
that person that you just paid PPC, or SEO, or banners or whatever to generate. So,
it’s multistep. Instead of just one shot – do a direct mail and get a guy in – you’re more
patient and willing to wait, and the numbers can be bigger.
Now obviously if you’re doing process promos now, they require a very low cost in order
to work or a very high price, high margin product. So if that’s the way you’re going, you’ll
want to test maybe other media that are equally as cheap, or you’ll want to test products
that are higher priced or have better margins. So there will be a lot to test there. If the
client is doing these in addition, you may want to consider doing these as well.
That’s kind of what I was talking about earlier with that health company. I went in there
and all they were doing was one-shot promos to bring in new customers. All they did was
direct mail. They would send out a promo – buy this supplement. People would buy the
supplement and then that person would be on their file. Every month they would receive
additional promotions.
We came in and one of the first things that we did, after we improved the response rate
on their one-shot promos, was we created a web presence that was a process acquisition
kind of campaign. In fact, I just got an email in my box from Human Events, and it’s that
client inviting me to get a special report on heart health. So we added this to it. If he’s
doing this, you’ll want to test this. If he’s doing this exclusively, you’ll definitely want to go
back the other way.
A lot of guys today are doing just the web. I just had an experience where I took a bunch
of my web promotions for a particular client and threw them over to my buddy, John
McCray, who is a great copywriter on our staff, and I said, “Here John, turn this into a
direct mail piece.” This was copy that was working on the web quite well. John did it and
gave that client the very best ROI he’s seen in the mail, he said, in five years by using
that copy over again. So, food for thought.
Test your offers and your offer strategy.
There are a lot of different offer strategies, and we’re going to go through some of them
here. Again, this is like a check-list, kind of an idea starter that you can use when you’re

-- 7 of 23 --

Makepeace Business System
Module 3 • Making A Big Splash
Transcript 	Page  of 23
The Total Package 	www.makepeacetotalpackage.com
looking at your client’s products.
See, isn’t this fun? Instead of somebody saying to you, “Look, I want a new direct mail
piece. I want you to write it about this, and I want it done in 30 days,” you’re able to go
in and you’re able to say, “Hey, why don’t we think outside of that box – why don’t we
just get outside of any box and take a look at the entire process and the entire marketing
model. Let’s see what we can do to hit home runs on five, six, or seven levels instead
of just, okay, so I wrote a better headline than the other guy.” So it gives you a lot more
chess pieces to play with.
Alright, different types of offers you can test.
• 	A loss leader or continuity offer
This is like what you see the Computer Professor do online. That’s where he offers you a
free lesson on how to run a particular program. You have to pay shipping and handling.
And what he doesn’t tell you on television is that you will then receive and be billed for an
additional lesson every month or so until you tell him to stop.
So he’s losing money on that first disc – actually he’s not because he’s charging an
outrageous shipping and handling fee. In fact, I was talking to a buddy the other day
and he goes, “That guy’s product isn’t computer lessons, his product is shipping and
handling.” [laughing] I think he gets $10 per order shipping and handling.
Anyway, that’s the loss leader continuity offer. It works particularly well on any kind of
broad market product where you have a huge prospect universe, where your product
is very cheap to deliver relative to the price that you are charging, and when it is like a
subscription-type of product – something you deliver every month or every week or over
some given period of time.
• 	The free trial preview continuity idea.
Think about Boardroom. They send you a promotion that says, “Hey look, here’s this
really great book on health or taxes or personal finance or whatever. We want you to
preview it free for 60 days. It’s a soft offer. Send no money now, just return this card and
we’ll send you the book. If you don’t like it, return it and owe nothing; otherwise, we’ll
invoice you for $29,” or whatever the price is.
It can also be applied in a continuity way where they say, “Okay, we want to send you this
free book or video or whatever to preview. If you like what you see, do nothing and you’ll
receive the next installment each month, and every month we’ll bang your card for the
appropriate amount.” So this is different in that there’s no shipping or handling charge up
front, and it can be applied very well to continuity programs as well as to one-shot books.
• 	The next one is a free product with negative options.
This is like what the record companies, book companies, and magazines do. They come

-- 8 of 23 --

Makepeace Business System
Module 3 • Making A Big Splash
Transcript 	Page  of 23
The Total Package 	www.makepeacetotalpackage.com
to you and say, “Select any seven books on this list or in this package. You know, tear out
the little stamp and stick it on the reply card. We’ll give it to you free, and then the only
string is from now on we’re going to send you a letter every month telling you what our
selection of the month is, like the Book Club Selection Of The Month. If you want it, don’t
do anything. We’ll send it and we’ll bill you. Otherwise, just tell us not to send it or select
an alternative.”
So that’s what a negative option is. They’re going to get it unless they tell you no, but they
have an opportunity to tell you no.
• 	Contest and sweepstakes.
This, of course, is the Publisher’s Clearing House model, but it also works really well
for renewals. I think the most powerful renewal steps that Phillips had in any of their
campaigns was a contest, a sweepstakes. For renewing your subscription, you were
entered into a sweepstakes where you could win a trip to Hawaii or whatever.
For companies with multiple product lines, like Phillips for example, where they had 12
or 13 newsletters, they would have one sweepstakes but it would be run for all of their
newsletters at once. So they’re sitting there with half-million subscribers or whatever the
number was, and one of them was going to Hawaii. The cost to the company was very
low relative to the effectiveness of the campaign.
These hardboiled investors, these old curmudgeons, you know these angry old white men
that subscribe to Phillips’ investment newsletters ate these sweepstakes up. I guess they
had a streak of larceny in them or something because they just loved the idea of copping
a free trip or a free computer or whatever. So always consider those, especially when
you’re looking at customer retention programs.
• 	Hard offer
That’s where you just simply say, “Here’s the product, here’s the price, buy it.” It can be
applied to books, subscriptions, or clubs. One of the things though that I want to point
out with a hard offer, and this is something we’ve just learned in the last year or so, there
is one sentence that you can add to your promotions – just one simple little sentence
that will just rocket your customer retention. It will cut the cost of renewal or customer
retention programs by 60, 70, 80%.
Just one little sentence that goes something like, after you’ve presented your price and
the offer, say, “Plus, as a courtesy to you, your subscription will be automatically renewed
from now on until you tell us to stop.” One little obscure mention.
If you’re selling health products, say supplements, and you’re buying a three-month
supply, just have one little line in there that says, “Plus as a courtesy to you, we’ll send
you a fresh three-month supply every 90 days until you tell us stop.”
All of a sudden, you’ve got automatic reorders happening all the time that was part of their

-- 9 of 23 --

Makepeace Business System
Module 3 • Making A Big Splash
Transcript 	Page 0 of 23
The Total Package 	www.makepeacetotalpackage.com
original agreement with you when they placed the order, and it’s a done deal. So a cool
little tip there. We’re using that now on everything that we do in the financial field, and it’s
just wonderful.
I added #7 because it’s basically also for a hard offer but for a single product. It works for
both, for a hard offer for multiple products, which would be catalogs or even multiproduct
mailings.
There’s something I’m wanting to try right now. I have a client, as many of you know,
in the financial field right now, and he’s growing like crazy. As a matter of fact, while
I’m doing this webinar, he’s doing one right now for 12,000 investors that we’ve been
promoting for the last week, with a member of the Rockefeller family, about trading the
Forex. I expect that we’ll do probably $2 or 3 million dollars as a direct result of that call
over the next week or two.
So there’s something that I want to do with him that I haven’t been able to sell him on yet
because in direct mail where you’re paying $600 to send out a thousand pieces – you’re
spending $600,000 to mail out a million pieces. When the stakes are that high, we’ve
found that you need a very simple offer. But when you’re on the web, we have three
product levels.
Let’s say we have three products, all of them dealing with currencies – how to trade
currencies. The first product is a monthly newsletter that sells for $99. The second one is
a weekly trading program that trades ETFs (exchange-traded funds) that sells for $700.
And then there’s a high-price product, a $25,000 product, that helps you trade options.
So I’m thinking, why not give people an offer, especially on the web, at some point in
their life cycle where they can tell us which of those services they’re most interested in.
Right now we’re looking at their past buying history and saying, “Well, this guy has never
bought anything from us, so we’re only going to offer him our cheapest product.”
Well, maybe the reason he’s never bought anything from you is because you’ve only
offered him your cheapest product, and he’s not interested in buying once-a-month advice
on currency investing. Maybe he would jump at a $700 service like crazy that helps him
trade currency ETFs.
See, there’s a false assumption there that just because he hasn’t bought anything so far,
he must be broke and must be a deadbeat. But he might be a multibillionaire and he’s
bored silly with ETfs, but he’d be really happy to spend $5,000 on a spot Forex service
or $2,500 on an option service. So you want to look at those assumptions. Anyway, I’m
getting sidetracked.
Test media
You’re going to want to test media. When I say media, there are different ways to go with
this. First of all, obviously, you’re going to want to test new selections in the current media
that he’s using. So if he’s primarily using direct mail, you’re going to want to take a look at

-- 10 of 23 --

Makepeace Business System
Module 3 • Making A Big Splash
Transcript 	Page  of 23
The Total Package 	www.makepeacetotalpackage.com
what lists he’s mailing.
First you’re going to want to ask, “Are we testing, or have we tested, all of the most
promising lists in that particular universe?”
And again I strongly recommend for your own edification, if you haven’t already done this,
go to the library and get a copy of the Standard Rate and Data Service for mailing lists
and study it. I think it’s fascinating. You’re looking at people making money hand-over-fist.
It tells you what they’re selling, how much they’re charging, and how many new names
they’re getting every month. There’s a lot of good information in there.
You can go to whatever category your partner is or your company is in, and you can look
at all the other companies that are selling in that same market niche. You can then ask
for the mailing list that that client has already tested, along with the promotions that were
sent to those files. Analyze which of these lists might really work well so we can get his
mailing quantity up and the new selections in the current media that he’s using.
There’s another way to go about this, too. That is to look at the niche that he’s mailing into
now and ask what other niches are so close to this one that we could probably sell well
into them as well.
For example, let’s say you have a newsletter on health. We looked at the people who
were the very first subscribers to those health newsletters, and we looked at the fact that
there’s a psychographic there. These are people who don’t trust the establishment. They
want to take control of their own life. They want to take control of their own future, right?
They don’t’ trust doctors.
Well gee, what other niche out there is kind of like that? The answer was investors,
people who subscribe to investment newsletters. They don’t trust Washington to handle
their money for them. They don’t trust the pundits on CNBC, most of them who own their
own hedge funds or mutual funds and have their own axe to grind. So they want to take
control of their own investments, right?
So I wonder if we tested this health package to people who feel that way about money, if
we might get a good response. And the answer is yes. That is why today I just mentioned
that I just received an email from a client of mine who is selling a health product, but he’s
sending it to a pollical magazine list, Human Events.
Be thinking about what other groups or prospects are like your current group, mostly
psychographically. They’ve demonstrated the same mindset that your current customers
have.
So that’s two ways to test in a medium that you’re already using. For example, direct
mail. Now you can see how that might work, for example, with television too. You’re doing
health promotions on do-it-yourself health and you say, “Gee, I wonder if this would work
on CNBC for the same reason and a financial channel.”

-- 11 of 23 --

Makepeace Business System
Module 3 • Making A Big Splash
Transcript 	Page 2 of 23
The Total Package 	www.makepeacetotalpackage.com
Next is to test new types of media. I’ve done a lot of this and it’s a little bit dicey, but when
it works, it is just dynamite. If it’s working well for you online, take it to direct mail. Same
copy, same offer, same product. If it’s working well for you in direct mail, consider taking it
to television. Look at other media.
I took a 16-page sales letter one time, cut it down into a two full page ads for USA Today.
The ad cost, if I remember I had $30,000 – I bought it through a remnant broker so I got
a good deal on it, but it was still fairly expensive that it was like a center spread in USA
Today.
Most of my 16-page sales letter, or a good chunk of it anyway, was in there and worked
like gangbusters. We ended up buying every slot we could not only in USA Today but also
other newspapers and tabloids for that product. So look at other types of media.
Another thing to think about there is video. Video is all the rage right now on the internet.
We’re all producing little videos for our websites. If I have a client in the health field, for
example, and he’s selling something that unclogs your arteries, I can do an animated
flash that actually shows the product working in his arteries. It can be a whole little 8 or 9
minute presentation.
There are infomercials on TV that are working gangbusters that are nothing more than the
same 8 or 9 minute presentations strung together three times. So you have the makings,
if it’s working well online, of something that can work really well as an infomercial.
Something to think about.
Alright, and then joint venture media. A lot of people forget to even think about this.
Your client gets 600 advertising impressions a day. He’s getting at a minimum direct
mail, email, and even outbound telemarketing contacts from companies that he’s done
business with in the past.
They’re contacting him on a regular basis. They’re also sending him bills, statements,
reminders of one kind or another and he’s visiting their websites. They can be your direct
competitors in some cases, in others, indirect competitors. In other cases, they just
happen to have the same prospect universe that you do. That gives you a couple of really
great opportunities.
In the context of testing media, the obvious opportunity is to go to one of these companies
and to do a deal to essentially wholesale your product to that company and allow them to
sell your product to the people they’re contacting anyway.
Believe me, a guy who’s doing billings or a guy does a billing to his file, that’s a cost to
him. He sees the cost of that bill being a negative. If you can go and in and basically say,
“You never have to pay for your billing again because I’ll cut your billing costs in half if
you’ll let me stick an insert in there,” you’d be surprised how many people will do that.
They’ll also be happy if they have a catalog, maybe, to include your product. Look for
companies that are talking to the same kinds of customers and structure some kind of a

-- 12 of 23 --

Makepeace Business System
Module 3 • Making A Big Splash
Transcript 	Page 3 of 23
The Total Package 	www.makepeacetotalpackage.com
test to see if you can be effective selling to those customers, basically piggy backing them
what they’re already doing.
They’re also shipping products by the way to those customers and getting your package
stuffer in their product and allowing them either to take a percentage of the sale, which is
beautiful for you because you have no capital risk, or paying them a fee up front for the
use of that space in their fulfillment package is a great way to go.
Test copy
Next, you can test copy. Here are just some of the things that I test a lot. Themes are
really big for me because I do write in the financial thing. Right now, for example, we have
a lot of things I could write about.
If I’m doing a direct mail piece, I can write about the real estate crash. I could talk about
the mortgage meltdown and all of the mortgage defaults and how that’s going to affect the
economy. I could talk about the crash of the credit markets, how loans just aren’t being
made today and how to make money on that. I can write about how the dollars being
gutted by the Fed and Congress to try to head off a recession.
I can talk about, and maybe sell something, on currencies, or I can talk about how
commodity prices are soaring worldwide and how to make money on that. I can talk about
the fact that China’s economy is going to grow 10.6% this year whereas ours is going to
contract, so why don’t you subscribe to our newsletter about investing in China.
If I pick theme A and another copywriter picks theme B and I write the best copy that
could possibly be written on theme A, but theme B resonates with the market better at
that given time, the other writer is going to win.
If you’re partnering with a client, it becomes incumbent on you to go into his customer
acquisition promotions and to make sure you’ve got the hottest themes, hottest ways
possible in the mail, all the time being tested.
Test Formats
I just tested a 6X9 envelope package against a bookalog, and the bookalog beat it by
30%. It’s worth a test. I also tested headlines at the same time. Headline A which was my
first choice lost. Headline B beat it by about 35%.
Opening copy should only be tested when you’re testing a headline with it. You want to
test things that matter. You don’t want to test colors. You don’t want to test typefaces. You
don’t want to test small differences.
You ask yourself, “ Could this change in the promotion generate one additional new
sale?” Well, if you ask that question about a color, nobody is going to say, “I’m going to
buy this product because this headline was red and not green.” Test things that really
matter.

-- 13 of 23 --

Makepeace Business System
Module 3 • Making A Big Splash
Transcript 	Page  of 23
The Total Package 	www.makepeacetotalpackage.com
Opening copy alone, no one’s going to read it unless the headline grabs them, so opening
copy/headline combinations are great tests. Premiums, pricing, payment options – is it a
bill me? Pay up front? Installment? There’s even a permutation that says, “You can pay
now but we’re not going to cash your check for 30 days.”
Look at the different payment options. A great way to get cues for testing there is to look
at who has used which payment options that you’ve already used on your file.
Test the Guarantee
We did a test not too long ago where we looked at your cancellations and refund
requests. We saw that we had a 60-day money-back guarantee. You get your money
back, 100% if you cancel within 60 days but anytime thereafter, you get a refund on the
remaining portion of your subscription.
We looked at the data. Of course, we had this big, huge spike in cancellations right
around day 58, 59 because those guys had made a mental note or maybe even stuck
something on the refrigerator that said, “Cancel this product on such and such a date.”
They got all of our free premiums. They got our 2 months worth of service from us and
then cancelled and got a full refund. Our question was, “Okay, so that was pretty fresh
in his memory if he just ordered 60 days ago. What if we tested a one-year guarantee
against that 60 days and see what happens. Wouldn’t people be less likely to remember
that the deadline for getting their money back was coming up?”
Bottom line is net/net, one year won hands down. We got a huge drop in cancellations.
They didn’t come. There was no spike, not even a bump at 60 days, and there was no
increase over the regular monthly cancellation rate at the end of the year. That meant
huge money for the client. Look at your guarantee. Look at the terms and test them.
Also look for urgency motivators.
Test Deadlines
Deadlines are great and testing them is important. A deadline is, “If you buy before April
31, you get a 50% discount or you get a free gift or something else.”
Scarcity is another one – we’re only selling 500 of these. That usually only works on very
high dollar things. There’s a way to make a scarcity sell work on lower dollar things. We
did it with Rodale and it’s called a Fast 50 Premium, where basically you say that the very
first 50 orders we get will get a free blender or just something ridiculous. This is on a $29
book.
That caused a lot of people to want to be in that first 50. I was always concerned that the
ones that didn’t get the blender would be ticked off and cancel, but it didn’t turn out to be
a problem.

-- 14 of 23 --

Makepeace Business System
Module 3 • Making A Big Splash
Transcript 	Page  of 23
The Total Package 	www.makepeacetotalpackage.com
ADDITIVE IMPROVEMENT
We talked about going online, adding an online component. I just want to say this again.
The cool thing about additive is yeah, there’s a burst of activity early on with a new
partner, but once you get these things handled, they make you money forever.
Here are some of the layers that you can add to your cake here. You can add an online
component; website, ezine, pay-per-click, make sure everything is optimized for the
search engines, for the spiders.
You can add product stuffers. Remember every single contact with a customer, I don’t
care what it is, is an opportunity to sell. When you ship a product, you should have
product stuffers in there. One of the best product stuffers we’ve ever used have been
discount coupons. For one client we even did a calendar. We gave them a calendar, a
beautiful calendar that you’d want to hang in your kitchen and when you open it up to
every single month, there would be like five coupons in there for a discount on different
products.
This was a health client, so month one was heart month or month two was joint health
month and month three was good digestion month or whatever. Then every month would
have coupons for the products the client had that kind of supported the theme for that
month.
People were using those coupons. It increased sales every single month when they
flipped that calendar over. That calendar went out with every order. Even just a simple
letter with a coupon is a great way to get additive improvement here in terms of revenues.
We talked about catalogs, excellent way to go.
Customer service scripting
I mentioned this, I think it was two months ago, on our webinar but I love doing this. I
just really enjoy it. I’ll call up a client’s toll-free number and I will put that poor customer
service person through their paces. If they only offer one- and two-year subscriptions, I’ll
tell them I want a four-year subscription.
If they say it takes two weeks to get my free gift, I want them tomorrow. Can I get them
FedEx? I’ll be nice but I’ll just ask every possible question I can. I’ve had customer
service departments hang up on me. I’ve had them tell me, “No, we only sell one- and
two-year subscriptions. You can’t buy a three-year subscription at any price.”
Man, I’m typing like crazy while I’m doing this, a report to my client, the company owner,
giving him ideas to improve his customer service. When I say scripting here, I don’t mean
scripting every kind of call they could ever get, but laying out broad parameters on how
to increase sales and deal with the most common kinds of questions and problems they
receive.

-- 15 of 23 --

Makepeace Business System
Module 3 • Making A Big Splash
Transcript 	Page  of 23
The Total Package 	www.makepeacetotalpackage.com
If you can imagine, here I am the copywriter who’s making money by driving people to
this 800 number, and then having the people on the 800 line be totally incompetent. I had
a good excuse for jumping all over that one.
Customer retention programs
It’s amazing how many really excellent direct mail and direct marketing companies I’ll
go into and I’ll ask them to show me their renewal program or their “We want you back”
program or special incentive for customers to order just because – at 11 months, just
before they become classified as inactive customers, and they show me stuff. Some of it’s
okay and some of it isn’t.
Then I ask them, “When was this done?” The answer is usually, three, four, five years
ago. Nobody’s watching it. Nobody’s testing it. Nobody’s identifying which steps in these
programs are working and which one’s aren’t, and yet that’s the main profit center of
many of these companies.
You sell somebody a one-year subscription and you’re doing it at break even, and then
you get whatever you spend in the mail to get that new subscriber, you get that back
hopefully. Then you send them a $15 welcome kit and then you fulfill on their subscription
for a year for $1.00 a month or whatever that costs.
You’re out $27. You’re in the hole $27. The whole profit center of the company is getting
that renewal ,and yet renewals always take a backseat. The reason is even though that’s
where the money is, the risk is on the front end and companies tend to be more afraid of
risk than they are excited about profits, which mystifies me but I can’t think of any other
explanation.
Why would they spend millions of dollars, enormous amounts of staff time developing
new controls to bring in new customers, but they don’t spend hardly any time or money
on the back end making sure that every step of their retention program is as sharp as it
possibly can be? That’s a big one.
Product ideas
You come in with new products, new ways to add revenue for your client. With product
ideas there’s a lot to think about. You’re talking to a particular company and you want
to expand his product line in some way. You know that for every new product you add,
that’s a whole new income stream, that’s a whole new profit center for the company. It’s
a natural way to really grow the company’s revenues and profits and to take a nice chunk
for yourself.
There’s several different ways here.
• 	The first one is enhancement.

-- 16 of 23 --

Makepeace Business System
Module 3 • Making A Big Splash
Transcript 	Page  of 23
The Total Package 	www.makepeacetotalpackage.com
That's taking a pre-existing product and adding some component to it that either makes
it more valuable or makes it more valuable so you can charge more or makes it more
desirable so you get a higher response rate.
A good example of that is a financial newsletter that comes out once a month. It's a print
newsletter. You get it in your mailbox. It sells for $99 a year but you want to enhance it so
you add email-trading recommendations on say currencies and commodities.
In addition to this once a month thing that's like your grand strategy and it's your main
portfolio and any monthly investment moves you need to make, you get these breaking
news, hot flash emails that say, "Buy this ETF now. Gold has just fallen. Great time to buy.
Get in there. The Euro is soaring against the dollar. Let's get into the Euro. Buy this ETF."
By doing that, we have two ways to go and we could test both of them. One is raise the
price of the service. Instead of $99, now that's $199 service. Let's try that. The other
way to do it is just say keep the price the same and say in your promotion, "Normally we
charge $400 for this email reco service, and you get it for free if you join now." Product
enhancement is a way to go.
• 	Next is extension.
That's where you take a current product line – in my case it would be supplements and
your client has a supplement that unclogs your arteries and it's going gangbusters. The
safest thing to do then would be to say, "Obviously, we're building a large list of people
who are concerned about their hearts. What are the other concerns about heart health
other than arteries?"
Inflammation which triggers heart attack and stroke, blood clots. Let's create products that
handle those other two problems for heart health. Then maybe, just for good measure,
throw in a co-enzyme Q-10 product which is for heart pumping action, the strength of your
heartbeat.
That gives us three new products that are all very low risk because they're in exactly the
same niche that's working so well for us right now.
The same could be true in the investment area. The guy's got a newsletter about how
to make money in tech stocks and you say, "Why don't we add another newsletter that's
kind of like the next step, and it's on how to make money and options on tech stocks." It's
simply the extension. Again, this is a very low risk way to go because you already have
something that's working in a particular niche.
• 	The next one is expansion.
That would be simply to take the product that you're already producing. This works
especially well for, or maybe even exclusively, for information products. You're delivering
a print newsletter. Let's reformat it and let people buy it as a podcast or let's produce it
in video every month and allow them to subscribe to a video service. They can watch it

-- 17 of 23 --

Makepeace Business System
Module 3 • Making A Big Splash
Transcript 	Page  of 23
The Total Package 	www.makepeacetotalpackage.com
online or they get a DVD.
You're already producing the content. You've already proven the niche. You already know
your marketing works. Let's look at other ways to package that content to create other
products that will be attractive to people in other groups.
One of the things to keep in mind there is depending on who you are, how old you are,
and where you are, you prefer to get your information in different kinds of formats. If
you're a young woman, you're more likely to want to get your information in nice glossy
color magazines. If you're an old curmudgeon like me, a print newsletter is just fine.
If you're a young techie guy, maybe you insist on getting your information on a blog or in
some other online format. The more ways that you can repackage that content, the more
products you'll have and the more market niches you'll be able to go to. That's expansion.
• 	Then there's intensification.
This is what we do when we take a $99 service that gives you once a month stock recos
and then say, "Yeah, but if you want weekly recommendations on exchange traded funds
to trade in this particular thing, currencies, commodities, whatever that's $795. You just
bought the $99 thing. Congratulations, you're a genius. It's a great product but do you
know that if you're treading more frequently in these markets, you can make a lot more
money. Okay, we've got this service that helps you trade ETFs. There's no leverage. The
return is great if you do it right. It's $795 a year but look at how much money you could
have made."
Then it intensifies even further by saying, "Since the dollar's crashing nonstop and the
Euros' going through the roof for seven years, it's kind of a no brainer. Why don't we
show you how to trade options on the Euro? Or even trade the Euro in the stock market
and we're going to charge you a fortune for that but that's okay because look how much
money you could make."
You're intensifying the service and at each level, of course, you get fewer and fewer
customers but you're making a lot more money because of the higher price. It's justified
because you're making them more money. You're sending them more alerts, sometimes
daily for example in some kinds of services. Intensification is a great way to go.
• 	Continuity and relationship prestige products and clubs
I have a client where we took ten of his services, bundled it into a club, a Chairmen's
Circle more or less, and sold it for $25,000. We made a lot of money. They also get
improved access to their guru, and celebrity has an appeal that's worth paying for to
people. They get greater access to the guru they expect, and they get everything he
publishes and it's $25,000. Again, it works great.
• 	Finally, JV partner products

-- 18 of 23 --

Makepeace Business System
Module 3 • Making A Big Splash
Transcript 	Page  of 23
The Total Package 	www.makepeacetotalpackage.com
This is kind of the reverse of what we were talking about earlier. Instead of just going to
people who have prospects in your same niche and say, "Would you sell my product?"
you use that relationship that you established earlier on to go to them and say, "I don’t
have a product to promote this month. I'd like to pump up my catalog or my website a little
bit. Why don't you wholesale your product to me? I'll sell out of your inventory. I'll have no
inventory costs, no product development costs and I'll just take X% of the money, and I'll
give you X% of the money and we'll both be happy."
Those are the things to look at there in the area of additive improvement.
EXPLOSIVE IMPROVEMENT
This is where I know that most of you are on to me. You know where I’m going with this
because I wrote about it not too long ago. I told the story of Dan Rosenthal, who went into
a client’s company and said, “What are you selling your people on the back end?”
He said, “Nothing. Renewals, that’s it.”
Dan said, “You’ve got 35,000 or 30,000 subscribers/customers right now and you’re not
really promoting anything to them on the back end. I think if we developed a product just
for them and because they know you, they love you, they trust you, it could be much
higher priced. Let’s offer that to these people.”
The guy says, “Okay.”
Dan says, “Okay, but since it’s my idea and I have to do all the work, I want 30% of the
money we generate.”
The client goes, “Oh, okay.”
Long story short; $10 million in sales over a period of about, I think, a week to 10 days.
Dan walks away with millions of dollars. The client walks away with millions of dollars. It
worked out great for everybody.
It’s amazing how many opportunities like that you’re going to find. I was talking to Bill
Guthy of Guthy-Renker several years ago. Their top product at the time was Vanna
White’s Tooth Whitening Kit.
I said, “You run these great infomercials with Vanna White and you sell all these tooth
whitening kits. What do you sell to your customers next?”
He said, “What do you mean?”
They were making so much money on the front end that the front end was occupying
100% of their time and they had never given a thought to what they were going to sell
these people in month two, or month three, or month four.

-- 19 of 23 --

Makepeace Business System
Module 3 • Making A Big Splash
Transcript 	Page 20 of 23
The Total Package 	www.makepeacetotalpackage.com
You’d just be knocked out by how many companies are like that. The cool thing is that it’s
the most successful companies that are like that. The one’s that are struggling a little bit,
they’re already trying to eek out every penny of profit they can. It’s the ones that are going
great guns and making money hand over fist. Those are the ones that tend to have the
biggest opportunity on the file.
Under the rubric of explosive improvement, we’re talking about customer file promotions.
Again, these are people who know you. They love you or your partner’s company. They
trust you and if you go to something in their field of interest, it brings value to their lives on
the same level or greater level than they’ve already paid you for you’re going to get huge
increases in sales in very huge periods of time. Again, here there are two ways to go
about this.
One shot programs or campaigns
Basically, when a file is new and they haven’t seen a lot of promotion, a one shot
campaign, anything that’s out of the ordinary, is going to produce tremendous results
but then that’s going to turn into a business for you and you’re going to be doing it every
single month, and the file will begin to become inured to it. They’ll get a little numb.
They’ve seen it. They’ve heard it before.
With one client who was in that situation, I said, “To hell with this. Send out one letter
and hope we do as well as we did the very first time that happened. Let’s plan a 60-day
campaign and just knock their socks off. We’re going to start with a letter and then we’ll
go to daily emails to them about that letter. And to our very best customers a FedEx
package.
“Then daily emails about that package or about that letter to drive them to the product.
Then we’re going to reformat the letter as a magalog. We’re going to send that out at the
beginning of month two. We’re going to make sure our newsletters and our ezines and
everything support this push. We’re going to focus intensively on this just for 60 days.”
Later we came in and we added online events, webinars, video briefings, teleseminars
and so forth. I can tell you that today, something like seven, eight, nine years after
Rosenthal went in with that client and created $10 million on his first promotion, we’re still
fielding promotions but they’re campaigns, much more intense, that are bringing in $10
million apiece. It’s doable.
Because of the internet, because of the low cost of customer contact online, I prefer
campaigns. I know that if a tired file or a file that hasn’t grown in a while is responding
extremely well to campaigns so much better than a one shot, I go with campaign strategy
on everything. You’re not thinking about just one promotion. You’re thinking about an
unfolding story.
We’re now into the part of this where obviously these are the things, this explosive
increment, this is what you’re going to want to do in the first 60 days. Any of you who
have been with me for a while have heard me talk about horizontal and vertical marketing

-- 20 of 23 --

Makepeace Business System
Module 3 • Making A Big Splash
Transcript 	Page 2 of 23
The Total Package 	www.makepeacetotalpackage.com
on a house file.
Vertical marketing is where you just hit the entire file. It doesn’t matter how long they’ve
been with you, how much they spend with you, what their recency or frequency or amount
of average purchase is. You bang everyone on the file with a promotion on a particular
product.
Usually the type of promotion you do is driven by some kind of shared experience or
event or qualification. On a vertical promotion, you’re banging the entire file. Everybody
on the file gets the same letter, same offer, same price, same everything.
Horizontal is when you stratify the file and you say, “Send this only to people who spend
$0 to $12 with me. Send this other letter to everyone that spent $12 to $25 with me,” and
so forth until you’ve covered the entire file.
Vertical promotions are driven maybe by product introduction or a holiday or a date.
Horizontal promotions are driven by the customer; how he’s qualified, maybe this is only
to people who have a birthday today or whose anniversary it is today.
If you weren’t on the call when we went into great depth on customer file marketing and
horizontal and vertical promotions, be sure to go and watch that video because I think it
will help you a lot. There are a lot of other things that have proven explosive for me over
the years. (Module 4, Makepeace Money Multiplier)
Company positioning
The company positioning has created some of the biggest sales explosions that I’ve ever
experienced in my career. Trying a new or different or celebrity spokesperson can create
an explosive result. The rest of these items on this slide; media, offer, pricing guarantee,
delivery mode, order mode, fulfillment, customer service, and joint ventures are some of
the things that I talk about earlier.
These can be applied to your house file. In terms of media, let’s get offline every once in
a while. Let’s go ahead and get him a phone call. Let’s go ahead and send him a FedEx
package or a first class letter.
Offer, same thing. Let’s try different offers with them or maybe stratify the file and offer
everybody who in the past has preferred soft offers, a soft offer. And offer everyone, who
in the past has built on a hard offer, a hard offer.
Pricing, same thing. Stratify the file by the price and so forth. You’ll see those items,
letters A through K in the PDF that you downloaded.
Now we’re down to the end here. We have our first 60 days. What would I typically do
with the companies that I work with?
1. 	First is going to be creating a powerful campaign for existing customers. I’m going

-- 21 of 23 --

Makepeace Business System
Module 3 • Making A Big Splash
Transcript 	Page 22 of 23
The Total Package 	www.makepeacetotalpackage.com
to take you through the product, the positioning, how to decide what kind of offer,
how to set your pricing, all of those kinds of things.
Notice that #1 is the only thing here that you need to actually execute in the first 60 days.
That’s really good because doing it and doing it right is going to take you 60 days of your
full time and attention.
I think you can set aside enough time to get these other things done too. Creating a
campaign is going to blow the doors off anything that your client has ever seen for his
house file. It’s going to cement the relationship in wonderful ways.
I’ve had clients give me cruises, gold coins, all kinds of wonderful things in thanks for
just doing that. That’s going to give you the credibility and the power and establish the
foundation for your relationship as we discussed. These next few things though are
things that will then take it to the next level and really set the tone for the next year you’re
working with that client.
2. 	The next thing is to create a comprehensive customer file marketing plan.
As I said, I have a video on that. If you didn’t watch that webinar, I strongly suggest that
you do. (Module 4, Makepeace Money Multiplier)
3. 	You want to create and improve customer retention materials.
On that, again, this is more a proposal or an idea document than it is actually doing the
work in the first 60 days. Basically, to do the idea document, get it approved, get it in the
pipeline so that it’s something you jump in as soon as you have this first promotion in the
bag.
4. 	Next, create customer service proposal.
Ambush the customer service department. Do what I suggested. Do everything that you
can to think of to drive them nuts. Find holes. Maybe you won’t find many. Maybe you’ll
find some you can drive a truck through but anything you find in there, think about your
client.
He spends hundreds of thousands, cash out of pocket, in most cases to acquire new
customers or to drive people to that 800 number so they can order a product, and if
there’s an employee sitting there who’s too lazy or just not bright enough to turn that call
into a sale, the client needs to know that.
In most cases, it’s not that they’re not bright enough or not lazy enough. It’s just they
haven’t been given direction. Ideas that you provide along those lines will be really
valuable.
5. 	Reduce delivery costs.

-- 22 of 23 --

Makepeace Business System
Module 3 • Making A Big Splash
Transcript 	Page 23 of 23
The Total Package 	www.makepeacetotalpackage.com
Look at ways that you can piggyback deliveries in the physical world. If you’re delivering
a product, is there a way that you could also deliver that guy’s newsletter that way in that
month? Is there a way to deliver his premiums online as PDFs instead of printing them
and mailing them and incurring that high cost?
Think about all of the ways that you might be able to save him money. There’s a lot to be
said there because some of this just has to do with spending smarter when he’s doing his
marketing. Some of it has to do with things that you can afford to do for free for this guy
because you have a piece of the back end. We’ll be talking about that in our next call.
6. 	Finally, and this is what a lot of people start with, but it should be the last thing that
you do in that first 60 days, is present the client with a new product development
plan.
The new product thing is pretty cool because once you’ve given him a whole bunch of
ideas and hopefully prioritized them in terms of which ones you think would work best
based on who his market is and what his marketing history is, then it’s off your desk.
Somebody else is going to create that product. You’re the marketing guy so somebody
else is going to create that.
Think about the combined effect here on your client at the end of the first 60 days. You
came in all guns blazing, created a promotion and put several million dollars into his
pocket.
Not only that – by doing that, you showed him a whole new business model in terms of
marketing to his house file where he can see that happening every month from now on
forever. You think he minds the fact that you made a million for doing that, 10% of what
you made him maybe?
Secondly, you came in with great ideas for doing this every month and for keeping his
customer longer and for making sure that every contact with customer service produces a
sale or produces higher average sale.
You saved him money in tangible ways that he can see. You’ve given him a vision of this
future with some red hot, new products in the pipeline. That’s my first 60 days.
Alright guys, we’re an hour and 30 minutes into our one hour call. I hope this has been
helpful for you. Thanks a lot. It was fun. I hope it was helpful. Take care. Bye-bye.

-- 23 of 23 --
